cards.front: Can you create an array of references in C++?
cards.back: No, you cannot directly create an array of references because references are not objects. Arrays can only hold objects.

cards.text: To create an array-like container of references, use {{c1::std::reference_wrapper}} which is defined in the {{c2::<functional>}} header
To create a std::array of references to ints, declare: {{c1::std::array<std::reference_wrapper<int>, 3>}} arr { x, y, z };

cards.front: What is the key behavior of std::reference_wrapper?
cards.back: std::reference_wrapper is a copyable object that behaves like a reference. It allows references to be stored in containers and can be implicitly converted to the referenced type.

cards.text: To access the underlying object from a std::reference_wrapper, use the {{c1::get()}} member function or rely on {{c2::implicit conversion}}

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.explanation: ref.get() returns a reference to x, so assigning to it modifies the original variable x.

cards.text: Instead of std::reference_wrapper<T>, you can use the helper function {{c1::std::ref()}} for shorter syntax
For const references, use {{c1::std::cref()}} which creates a std::reference_wrapper to a const object

cards.question: What's a cleaner way to write this?
cards.code:
int x=1, y=2, z=3;
std::array<std::reference_wrapper<int>, 3> arr { x, y, z };
cards.explanation: Using CTAD and std::ref() makes the code more concise and readable.

cards.front: Do you always need to call get() to access the value in std::reference_wrapper?
cards.back: No, std::reference_wrapper has an implicit conversion operator that allows it to be used directly as the referenced type in most contexts.

cards.explanation:
Use std::reference_wrapper when you need:
  • To store references in containers (arrays, vectors)
  • To pass references through standard library algorithms
  • To create copyable reference-like objects
Avoid when simple references or pointers would suffice.
examples: std::array arr { std::ref(x), std::ref(y) };  // Array of refs
